_note: Original, owned room descriptions for this remaster — our prose, in the original's tone, keyed to the real rooms. NOT Interplay's R*.BIH text. Ships freely (no BYO files).

desc.R1: A low bar for professional expatriates, thick with smoke and the reek of synth-beer. You could drink here a week and never hear two words of Japanese. A PAX booth glows on the back wall; Ratz works the taps with a whining pink prosthetic arm.
desc.R2: Ninsei at street level — wet neon, noodle steam, and a crowd that moves like one tired animal. A Chiba beat-cop watches you from a doorway, deciding whether you're worth the paperwork.
desc.R3: A cramped booth walled in monitors. A Compu-Judge scowls from the big screen while a Compu-Lawyer flickers on the small one, both running on the same indifferent code.
desc.R4: A clinic for new flesh and chrome. Jars line the shelves, the air smells of antiseptic and money, and the surgeon's smile never reaches the eyes that are pricing your organs.
desc.R5: The strip widens under a dead advertising sign. Stalls hawk grilled things on sticks and the gutters run with rainbow runoff from the clinics.
desc.R6: Fluorescent, sticky, and open all night. A couple of Chiba cops nurse coffee in the corner booth, watching the door a lot harder than their crullers.
desc.R7: A coffin rack of capsule rooms stacked to the ceiling. The clerk behind the cage doesn't look up. One of these coffins is yours, at least until the rent comes due.
desc.R8: The cowboys' bar. Console jockeys and burned-out hustlers trade war stories under a haze of smoke, and nobody here asks your real name.
desc.R9: A blank corporate frontage, all mirrored glass and quiet menace. Maas doesn't advertise; if you're standing at this door, you already know too much.
desc.R10: The cabin of a suborbital hop, all worn acceleration couches and recycled air. Somewhere far below, the lights of the sprawl smear past the window.
desc.R11: A Rastafarian cluster welded out of salvage and faith, dub bass thrumming through the bulkheads like a second pulse. The air is sweet with smoke and zero-gravity calm.
desc.R12: Racks of bootleg ice-breakers and skillsofts behind bulletproof glass. Larry watches you over a paperback, thumbing the same page he was on an hour ago.
desc.R13: Outside the chip parlors, where 'microsofts' — slivers of canned expertise — change hands for the price of a kidney. Touts murmur skills you could wear behind your ear.
desc.R14: A narrow run of pawn fronts and shuttered stalls. Behind one grille, a hand-lettered sign promises cash for hardware: no questions, no refunds.
desc.R15: The flophouse end of Ninsei, where the neon's half burnt out and the puddles never quite dry. Someone is always asleep, or pretending, in the next doorway.
desc.R16: The block outside the Gentleman Loser, thick with cowboys cadging credits and corp scouts pretending they aren't recruiting.
desc.R17: A cleaner stretch of street — too clean. The Maas frontage hums with the particular silence of a place that watches back.
desc.R18: The strip frays toward the launch fields. The crowd thins, the signs switch to departure boards, and behind the gantries the sky actually shows stars.
desc.R19: Chiba's launch terminal, all scuffed ceramic and customs drones. Orbital shuttles board here for Freeside and the high frontier — papers and credits permitting.
desc.R20: The cramped interior of a tug stitched from a century of salvage. A Zionite pilot rides the couch with his eyes closed, listening to the dub and the hull.
desc.R21: The approach to the Tessier-Ashpool spindle — grown, not built, all carved stone and old-money rot at the cold dark end of Freeside.
desc.R22: Inside the spindle, corridors twist like a nautilus shell. The air is centuries stale, heavy with the boredom of a family that refused to die.
desc.R23: A nest of the nihilist mercenaries who treat infowar as performance art. Mimetic polycarbon shimmers where a figure ought to be; you're being watched by people who work hard not to be seen.
desc.R24: Low light, warm steam, and a masseuse in a metallic robe who reads you faster than any cop. Whatever you came in tense about, you'll leave having said too much.
desc.R25: Glass cases of dead men's hardware. Shin keeps your impounded deck behind the counter and, by the set of his jaw, something heavier underneath it.
desc.R26: A pool of sodium light where one of Lonny Zone's girls works the corner, and a streetlamp flickers like it's trying to remember something.
desc.R27: Past a wall of import crates, an old man in a wool suit smiles from behind an antique desk. The air smells of ginger, and of money that has been around far too long.
desc.R28: Another shuttle cabin, identical to the last — the airline's idea of luxury is that you can't tell which flight you're on.
desc.R29: The arrival concourse of the orbital pleasure spindle. Tourists in paper slippers gawp at the curve of the floor while security pretends to smile.
desc.R30: The dock's outer ring, all handrails and warning placards in nine languages. Past the viewport, Freeside turns slow against the dark.
desc.R31: Outside Lonny Zone's place, where holographic touts shimmer in every doorway selling stims, sims, and dreams by the hour.
desc.R32: Inside, the walls run with shifting holograms while the proprietor sells simstim fantasies to anyone whose nervous system can take the bandwidth.
desc.R33: A facade of orbital marble and armored glass. The Bank of Berne keeps its money in vacuum and its secrets somewhere worse.
desc.R34: A hushed cathedral of finance. The tellers move like clergy, and the security ICE behind the walls is anything but quiet underneath the calm.
desc.R35: A corner office with a real-wood desk and a view of nothing in particular. The manager's smile is a transaction waiting to clear.
desc.R36: A dim arcade-bar where the machines are older than the patrons. Cigarette burns and spilled beer map the carpet like a city seen from orbit.
desc.R37: The block outside the arcade, loud with cabinet jingles leaking through the door and a busker running cracked synth through a blown amp.
desc.R38: A respectable-looking stretch, the kind with actual storefronts. Asano Computing's window glows with hardware that comes with warranties and serial numbers.
desc.R39: Edo's block, where the gray market spills onto the pavement and a hand-painted sign promises decks 'cheaper than that pig Asano.'
desc.R40: Edo fixes decks, sells gray-market chrome, and asks no questions. Half-gutted rigs hang from the ceiling like smoked hams — none of them, he'll cheerfully admit, cyberspace-capable.
desc.R41: A discreet Swiss-orbital front for money that prefers anonymity. The lobby is all soft light and softer threats.
desc.R42: The bank's armored entrance, flanked by potted plants and a security drone that tracks you with the patience of something that has all night.
desc.R44: A clean-lit storefront of legitimate gear, the kind with warranties and serial numbers. You feel conspicuous just standing in the doorway.
desc.R45: A checkpoint block patrolled by a Cop-issue security drone, its sensor cluster sweeping the crowd for the wrong kind of hurry.
desc.R46: A cowboy hangout dressed up as a noodle joint, where deals get cut over bowls nobody eats and the booths run better encryption than most banks.
desc.R47: The vault floor, deep in armored quiet. Rows of deposit drawers gleam under hard light, each one a secret somebody paid to forget.
desc.R49: A block gutted by fire, the shell of a tower hollowed to black ribs. Squatters' fires flicker in the upper floors, and the wind carries ash and old smoke.
desc.R50: Not a place so much as the memory of one — pale sand and a flat grey sea rendered at the ragged edge of the matrix, where dead cowboys are said to wash up and constructs go to think.
desc.R51: The lobby of Fuji Electric's Chiba tower, all brushed steel and recessed light, where the receptionist is almost certainly armed.
desc.R52: A corporate checkpoint bristling with scanners and bored menace. The guard's eyes flick from your face to a screen and back, doing math you won't like.
desc.R53: A storefront of curved teal tile and clean fluorescent calm, selling the kind of biotech that comes with a non-disclosure agreement.
desc.R54: A corporate arcade of glass storefronts and filtered air, where the foot traffic wears suits and the cameras outnumber the people.
desc.R55: Deeper into the high-tech district, the storefronts give way to lobbies with no signs — the kind of buildings that don't want walk-in trade.
desc.R56: The headquarters of the entertainment leviathan, a wall of screens broadcasting its own importance. Somewhere inside, a stolen construct dreams of being remembered.
desc.R57: Hosaka's corporate tower, severe and gleaming. The lobby ICE watches you the way a shark watches a swimmer who hasn't noticed it yet.
desc.R58: The Musabori headquarters, all black glass and quiet money. The doors open only for the right credentials, and yours are being checked right now.
